figures.gloss: The uncreated, incomprehensible source of all existence. Rather than a personal god, Gichi-manidoo is the ultimate cosmic animating principle—the 'Great Mystery'—that breathed life into the universe, leaving the active management of the world to lesser manidoog (spirits).
figures.facet: It is fundamentally non-anthropomorphic and transcends physical form. It does not demand worship or intervene directly, but permeates all things as the underlying, unifying spiritual fabric of reality.
figures.provenance: Basil Johnston, 'Ojibway Heritage' (1976); Frances Densmore, 'Chippewa Customs' (1929).

figures.gloss: The shape-shifting, semi-divine son of the West Wind and a human mother. He taught the Anishinaabeg how to hunt, use fire, and survive, while simultaneously acting as a vain, foolish trickster whose comic misadventures physically shaped the Great Lakes landscape.
figures.facet: He is neither fully divine nor fully human, embodying the paradoxical nature of existence. His power lies not in supreme wisdom, but in an adaptable, flawed resilience that perfectly mirrors the human struggle.
figures.provenance: Basil Johnston, 'Ojibway Heritage' (1976); A. Irving Hallowell, 'The Role of Conjuring in Saulteaux Society' (1942).

figures.gloss: A fearsome, dragon-like feline with copper horns and a spiked tail that rules the lakes and underworld. It commands whirlpools and water spirits, guarding the vast copper deposits of the region and demanding appeasement from humans for safe aquatic passage.
figures.facet: It represents the terrifying, unpredictable peril of deep water. Uniquely, it is the active custodian of terrestrial wealth, making it an ambivalent figure of both deadly chaos and material power.
figures.provenance: Theresa Smith, 'The Island of the Anishnaabeg' (1995); Norval Morrisseau, 'Legends of My People, The Great Ojibway' (1965).

figures.gloss: Massive, eagle-like sky beings whose beating wings create thunder and whose blinking eyes flash lightning. They migrate north in the spring to bring life-giving rain, engaging in a perpetual, elemental war against the underwater panthers to protect the surface world.
figures.facet: They are not distant meteorological phenomena but localized, migratory protectors. Their seasonal arrival signals the renewal of life and acts as an active, conscious defense of humanity against chthonic monsters.
figures.provenance: Theresa Smith, 'The Island of the Anishnaabeg' (1995); Basil Johnston, 'Ojibway Heritage' (1976).

figures.gloss: An exclusive, graded initiatory society of spiritual healers and record-keepers. Members utilize sacred birchbark scrolls and complex botanical knowledge to cure ailments, ensuring the continuation of Anishinaabe cosmology, ethics, and physical health through rigorous ceremonial apprenticeship.
figures.facet: It systematizes shamanic ecstasy into an organized, academic curriculum. Healing is achieved not solely through solitary vision quests, but through formalized peer transmission and the meticulous archival of knowledge on physical media.
figures.provenance: W.J. Hoffman, 'The Midewiwin or Grand Medicine Society of the Ojibwa' (1891); Michael Angel, 'Preserving the Sacred' (2002).

figures.gloss: Following the great flood that destroyed the old world, the humble muskrat dove deeper than the stronger animals. Though he drowned in the attempt, he successfully retrieved a single paw-full of mud that was placed on a turtle's back to grow into the new earth.
figures.facet: Creation is achieved through the ultimate sacrifice of the smallest, least capable creature. It embeds a deep ethic of humility, proving that cosmic survival depends on the courage of the seemingly insignificant.
figures.provenance: Basil Johnston, 'Ojibway Heritage' (1976); Ignatia Broker, 'Night Flying Woman' (1983).

figures.gloss: The beloved younger brother of Wenabozho who was drowned by jealous underwater spirits. Resurrected but unable to return to the world of the living, he was appointed the ruler of the afterlife, guiding the souls of the dead to the spirit village in the West.
figures.facet: He is not a sinister jailer of the damned, but a tragic, compassionate sibling whose unjust death instituted the necessity of mortality. He ensures the dead have a familiar, loving sovereign.

figures.gloss: Wenabozho's grandmother in the cycle — in some tellings a fallen daughter of the moon — who raises her orphaned, impulsive grandson upon the earth (aki) and provides the roots, medicines, and counsel he needs to survive and to shape the young world. Popular retellings sometimes blur her into 'Grandmother Earth' outright, but in Johnston's telling she is a personal ancestor who dwells on the earth, not its personification — the earth itself is aki, not Nookomis.
figures.facet: She is an enduring, stabilizing maternal presence set against the volatile heroics of the younger generation — grandmother and counselor, grounded in botanical and practical wisdom, rather than a goddess of the ground itself.

figures.gloss: An ice-hearted cannibal giant born of winter famine, forever hungry no matter how much it devours because its body grows with every meal. A human overtaken by greed, selfishness, or the desperation of starvation can themselves turn wiindigoo — the gravest spiritual affliction in Anishinaabe moral thought.
figures.facet: Uniquely among the roster's monsters, the wiindigoo is not a separate species to be fought off but a moral failure state any person can fall into — it collapses the distinction between monster and neighbor, making the ethic of sharing in famine a literal defense against transformation.
figures.provenance: Basil Johnston, 'The Manitous: The Spiritual World of the Ojibway' (1995); A. Irving Hallowell, 'The Role of Conjuring in Saulteaux Society' (1942); Lou Marano's 'windigo psychosis' debate in the ethnographic literature is noted but not adjudicated here.

figures.gloss: A divinatory ceremony in which a jaasakiid (conjurer) is bound and enclosed inside a small pole-lattice lodge that begins visibly shaking as manidoog arrive, answering the community's questions in audible, distinct voices heard emerging from the structure.
figures.facet: The evidentiary rite of the tradition's spirit ecology: unlike vision-quest trance that only the specialist experiences, the shaking tent produces public, sense-perceptible proof — a moving structure and audible voices — that outside observers, including skeptical ethnographers, repeatedly struggled to explain away.
figures.provenance: A. Irving Hallowell, 'The Role of Conjuring in Saulteaux Society' (1942).

figures.gloss: A sequence of seven prophesied eras structuring Anishinaabe history: the first fires narrate the migration from the Atlantic coast to the Great Lakes following the miigis shell, later fires foretell the arrival of light- and dark-skinned strangers and near-total loss of the old ways, and the seventh fire poses a choice between the destruction of a wrong path and the kindling of an eighth, eternal fire of renewal.
figures.facet: It frames colonization itself as a foreseen stage rather than a rupture, giving the tradition a historical narrative that runs from a specific geographic migration through the present crisis to a still-open future outcome, decided by living choices rather than fixed fate.
figures.provenance: Edward Benton-Banai, 'The Mishomis Book: The Voice of the Ojibway' (1988).

figures.gloss: A radiant cowrie shell that rose from the water to guide the ancestral migration from the Atlantic coast westward to the Great Lakes, and which figures in Midewiwin origin narratives as the vehicle by which the sacred medicine and the migration route were both revealed.
figures.facet: It ties two of the tradition's central narratives — the Seven Fires migration and the Midewiwin's own founding — to a single small, concrete object, making an abstract prophetic history and an initiatory society share one origin-emblem.
figures.provenance: William W. Warren, 'History of the Ojebway People' (1885); Edward Benton-Banai, 'The Mishomis Book' (1988).

figures.gloss: Patrilineal clan identities — crane, loon, bear, marten, and others — descending from original animal-beings, structuring marriage prohibitions, social duties, and political voice across Anishinaabe communities. The English word 'totem' derives from this term.
figures.facet: It is the tradition's structural pillar with no cosmological drama attached: unlike the trickster cycle or the flood narrative, the doodem system does its work quietly, through kinship law rather than myth, governing whom one may marry and who speaks for the community in council.
figures.provenance: William W. Warren, 'History of the Ojebway People' (1885); Basil Johnston, 'Ojibway Heritage' (1976).

figures.gloss: Small, hairy-faced beings said to dwell within riverbank rock, encountered by travelers along the water's edge and occasionally by Midewiwin practitioners, with whom they are sometimes said to exchange gifts or medicine knowledge.
figures.facet: They are the tradition's other-than-human neighbors rather than its monsters or gods: small-scale, place-bound, and capable of ordinary reciprocal exchange with humans, in contrast to the cosmic stakes carried by Mishipeshu or the Thunderbirds.
figures.provenance: Basil Johnston, 'The Manitous: The Spiritual World of the Ojibway' (1995).

figures.gloss: The West Wind manidoo, father of Wenabozho by a human mother. Their relationship is fraught: father and son come into unresolved conflict, sometimes battling across the width of the world, in one of the Wenabozho cycle's major episodes.
figures.facet: He supplies the cycle's one instance of a distant, half-absent divine father, against whom Wenabozho's own hybrid, unstable identity as 'neither fully divine nor fully human' is measured and explained.

edges.note: Intra-tradition: The demigod creator and the humble animal work as a team; Wenabozho has the magic to expand the earth, but only the Muskrat's sacrifice can retrieve the foundational mud.
Intra-tradition: The sky-dwelling Thunderbirds and the chthonic Underwater Panther are locked in a perpetual, balancing war of elemental forces across the boundary of the water's surface.
Intra-tradition: The two brothers divide the cosmos, with Wenabozho shaping the world of the living and Jiibayaabooz ruling and comforting the realm of the dead.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are incomprehensible, omnipresent Great Mysteries that animate the universe without being strictly anthropomorphized.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the infinite, unmanifested source of reality that is too vast to be conceptualized or directly interacted with by humanity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are the ultimate, formless cosmic principles that undergird the multiplicity of the visible world and its lesser, active deities.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are massive, storm-bringing Thunderbird spirits of the Plains and Woodlands who violently battle horned aquatic serpents.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Shared archetype of giant avian sky-beings whose physical movements directly generate the meteorological phenomena of thunder and lightning.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are formidable, horned underwater monsters that embody the chaotic peril of deep water and serve as the eternal enemies of thunder beings.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are deeply feared, destructive serpent-feline monsters associated with the unpredictable violence of storms and deep lakes.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A shared North American mechanism where an unassuming animal dives into a primordial sea to retrieve the speck of mud that becomes the earth.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the first mortal to die, whose tragic death establishes the path to the afterlife and who subsequently becomes its benevolent, sovereign guide.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are beloved figures murdered by jealous supernatural rivals, who cannot return to the living world and thus become the sympathetic lords of the dead.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are cultural trickster heroes whose vanity and selfish schemes paradoxically shape the physical landscape and teach humanity moral boundaries through failure.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are culturally beloved, semi-divine trickster-heroes who rely on cunning rather than raw power to steal boons for humanity and alter the geography.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are personifications of the earth as an ancient, sustaining mother-figure who directly provides the agricultural and botanical necessities for human survival.

The thunder-being-versus-underwater-power opposition is a widely shared Great Lakes/Eastern Woodlands motif; Héno's serpent-battling role rhymes with Mishipeshu's adversarial thunderbirds without documented direct descent.
Intra-tradition: the wiindigoo's all-consuming greed is the direct violation of the hunting and sharing ethic Wenabozho's own teachings establish for the Anishinaabeg.
Intra-tradition: Midewiwin healers are the recognized specialists called upon to treat or prevent the onset of wiindigoo transformation before it completes (Hallowell 1942).
Intra-tradition: the shaking tent is the recognized rite through which manidoog — the lesser spirits Gichi-manidoo leaves to manage the world — come and speak directly.

Intra-tradition: the tradition's two principal specialist institutions — the individual conjurer's divinatory tent and the graded Grand Medicine Society — operate as parallel, sometimes overlapping, technologies of ecstatic practice.
Intra-tradition: the shell is the guiding emblem of the westward migration the Seven Fires narrate — the physical object and the prophetic sequence are a single tradition.
Intra-tradition: the miigis shell figures directly in Midewiwin origin narratives as the vehicle of the medicine's revelation (Warren 1885).

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: two distinct North American Indigenous prophetic traditions each stage a coming choice-point between destruction and a renewed, restored world — independently attested, not descended one from the other.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: two North American kinship systems, unrelated by descent, that both root clan identity and social obligation in inherited animal-ancestor crests.
Intra-tradition: Memegwesiwag are said to occasionally transmit medicine knowledge directly to Midewiwin practitioners (Johnston 1995).
Intra-tradition: father and son, already named in Wenabozho's own roster gloss ('son of the West Wind') — their unresolved conflict is a major episode of the cycle (Johnston 1976).
Cross-tradition: unrelated graded men's/medicine societies that transmit secret ritual knowledge through successive initiatory ranks, structurally parallel with no claimed contact.
the great sky-bird whose natural enemy is the serpent or underwater monster, a widespread structural pairing independently arrived at in South Asia and the Great Lakes.

Both name an ultimate animating power now also identified with the Christian God by their own communities, but no textual equation as specific as the Lakota Wakan Tanka/NAC bridge is documented for Gichi-manidoo.

dossier.essence: The traditional religion of the Anishinaabe (Ojibwe/Chippewa, with their Odawa and Potawatomi kin), an Algonquian-speaking people of the Upper Great Lakes in the United States and Canada. It centers on Gichi-manidoo, the Great Mystery, and a world thick with manidoog — other-than-human 'persons' inhabiting animals, weather, water, and place — and remains a living tradition, often interwoven with Christianity.
dossier.cosmos: Reality is a layered, animate cosmos: a sky world, the earth resting on a great turtle's back, and a watery underworld, with the Thunderbirds above and the horned underwater panther (Mishipeshu) below locked in balancing enmity. The present earth was remade after a flood when the humble muskrat dove for a speck of mud — the widespread North American earth-diver motif — and the trickster-transformer Nanabozho (Wenabozho) shaped its features. At death the soul is said to travel a westward road of the dead (the 'ghost road,' often identified with the Milky Way) to a spirit village; the living remain enmeshed in reciprocal obligation with the manidoog, sought through dreams and the vision fast.
dossier.practice: Religious life turns on individual vision-seeking (fasting for a guardian manidoo), dreams, tobacco (asemaa) offerings, the sweat lodge, and the drum, organized by a system of clans (doodemag). Its most distinctive institution is the Midewiwin, the graded 'Grand Medicine Society' of initiated healers who transmit botanical medicine, songs, and cosmology and record mnemonics on incised birchbark scrolls. Ritual specialists also include the jaasakiid, the 'shaking-tent' conjurer who consults the spirits.
dossier.canon: An oral corpus — the aadizookaanag (sacred myths, told in winter) and dibaajimowinan (accounts) — supplemented by the Midewiwin's birchbark scrolls (wiigwaasabakoon), which are mnemonic records of songs and rites rather than scripture.
dossier.reflects: It foregrounds the organization of shamanic healing into a graded, knowledge-keeping institution — vision and cure systematized as a lodge with a curriculum and its own archival medium.
sources: Michael Angel, 'Preserving the Sacred: Historical Perspectives on the Ojibwa Midewiwin' (2002)
A. Irving Hallowell, 'Ojibwa Ontology, Behavior, and World View' (1960)
